title: Audience-Aware Prompting
concept: Specifying the reader (a beginner, an executive, a fellow engineer) to control explanation depth.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: If you ask an AI to 'act as a doctor from the 1800s,' can that persona give it real medical knowledge doctors didn't have back then?

options.text: No — a persona changes tone and style, but can't inject facts or knowledge the model doesn't actually have
Yes — pretending to be from a certain era automatically gives the AI that era's real knowledge
Yes — but only if you also say 'please' in the prompt

exercises.explain: Right! Personas are a costume, not a knowledge upgrade. They shape how the AI sounds, not what it actually knows or the facts it can access. That's the key limit we covered last lesson.

cards.title: Same News, Two Very Different Chats
cards.body: You'd explain 'the WiFi is down' very differently to your little cousin than to your company's IT director. Same fact, totally different words — because the LISTENER changes what they need to hear.

cards.title: What Is Audience-Aware Prompting?
cards.body: It means telling the AI WHO will read the answer — a beginner, a busy executive, a fellow engineer — so it picks the right vocabulary, depth, and examples. Same facts, different delivery.

cards.title: One Cake, Different Slices
cards.body: A birthday cake is the same cake for everyone, but you'd cut a tiny slice for a toddler and a big slice for a hungry teen. Facts are the cake; audience level decides the slice size.

cards.title: One Topic, Three Readers
cards.body: Prompt: 'Explain how batteries work to a curious 10-year-old.' → simple words, a toy-car analogy. Change it to 'explain to a mechanical engineer' → you get terms like electrodes, ions, and voltage without a basic definition of electricity.

cards.title: Myth: Simpler Is Always 'Dumbed Down'
cards.body: Explaining something for a beginner isn't lying or oversimplifying wrongly — it's choosing which true details to include. A good beginner explanation is still 100% accurate, just less detailed than an expert one.

cards.title: Beginner vs. Expert Explanation
cards.body: Same true topic, two very different deliveries — because the prompt named a different reader each time.

left.text: 'Explain black holes to a curious kid.' → 'Imagine a vacuum cleaner so strong not even light can escape!'

right.text: 'Explain black holes to an astrophysicist.' → 'Discusses event horizons, singularities, and spacetime curvature directly.'

exercises.prompt: Which prompt is the clearest example of audience-aware prompting?

options.text: 'Explain photosynthesis to a 7-year-old using a simple analogy, avoiding technical terms.'
'Tell me everything you know about plants.'

exercises.explain: Option B directly names the reader (a 7-year-old) and gives instructions matched to that reader's needs (simple analogy, no jargon). That's the core move of audience-aware prompting: naming WHO reads it to set the depth.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Specifying the audience only changes vocabulary difficulty, and has no effect on which examples or analogies the AI chooses.
exercises.explain: False — audience also shapes the examples and analogies used. A kid might get a toy-car analogy for batteries, while an engineer gets a technical diagram-style explanation with electrodes and voltage. Audience affects the whole package, not just word difficulty.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the audience-aware tip.
exercises.sentence: Naming the reader as a beginner, executive, or engineer controls the explanation's depth, not the underlying facts.
exercises.explain: Audience-aware prompting controls how deep or simple the explanation goes — the depth — while the actual facts stay accurate and unchanged.

exercises.prompt: A teacher asked two students to explain gravity for a 6-year-old. Which explanation was most likely AI-generated from a generic 'explain gravity' prompt with NO audience specified?

options.text: 'Gravity exerts an attractive force proportional to mass, causing objects to accelerate toward Earth's center at approximately 9.8 meters per second squared.'
'Gravity is like an invisible hand that pulls everything down to the ground, like when you drop your toy and it falls instead of floating away!'
'Gravity is why things fall down instead of floating up — like a magnet pulling toys toward the floor.'

exercises.explain: Option A uses technical vocabulary (proportional, accelerate, meters per second squared) that a 6-year-old wouldn't understand — a telltale sign the AI wasn't told who the audience was, so it defaulted to a general, technical explanation instead of a kid-friendly one.

exercises.prompt: You're writing one prompt to explain 'how vaccines work' three separate times: once for a curious 8-year-old, once for a hospital board of executives, and once for a team of immunologists. What's the SMARTEST way to do this with one base prompt template?

options.text: Write one single explanation and copy-paste it to all three groups unchanged
Keep the core facts about vaccines the same, but change the 'explain this to a [X]' instruction each time to reset vocabulary, depth, and examples
Give the AI a different persona each time, like 'you are a nurse' or 'you are a CEO', and skip mentioning the audience
Ask the AI to guess the reader's age and job from the topic alone

exercises.explain: The winning move is separating WHAT is true (vaccine facts, which shouldn't change) from HOW it's delivered (vocabulary, depth, examples, which should flex per reader). Naming the audience explicitly each time — kid, executive, immunologist — controls the depth dial precisely. Option A ignores the reader completely. Option C confuses persona (who's speaking) with audience (who's listening) — you need audience info regardless of the speaker's persona. Option D leaves it to guesswork, which is unreliable. Audience-aware prompting is one of your most reliable levers for getting explanations pitched at exactly the right level.
